title.en: Hybrid RAG Architectures: Data Here, Models There

description.en: Design retrieval-augmented generation when the corpus lives in one cloud (or on-prem) and the best model lives in another — placement patterns, residency-aware pipelines, and the egress arithmetic of RAG.

story_variants.en:
## The Corpus and the Model Live Apart

A European insurer wants an internal assistant over forty years of policy documents. The documents live in an EU-region data platform on the anchor cloud, classified confidential, and the residency rules from earlier lessons apply with full force. The model the business wants sits on a *different* cloud's AI platform. Welcome to **hybrid RAG** — the defining integration problem of enterprise GenAI, where everything this track has taught (gravity, residency, egress, identity, latency) converges on one pipeline.

## RAG, Deconstructed for Placement

RAG is not one workload; it is five, each with its own placement constraints:

1. **Ingestion & chunking** — batch compute over the raw corpus. Wants to live *next to the data*.
2. **Embedding generation** — model inference over every chunk (and re-runs on every embedding-model upgrade — budget for that, it is never once). Sends *full document content* to wherever the embedding model runs: a residency decision, not a technical detail.
3. **Vector index** — the searchable derivative. Inherits source classification (residency lesson: derived data carries its parent's obligations — embeddings can be partially inverted, so treat them as sensitive as their source).
4. **Retrieval** — low-latency query-time search. Must sit near the *user-facing app*.
5. **Generation** — the LLM call, carrying the query plus only the retrieved top-k chunks.

> **Callout:** The pivotal insight for hybrid design: **generation only needs the retrieved snippets, not the corpus.** Shipping 5 KB of context per query across a cloud boundary is routine; shipping (or re-embedding) a 40-TB corpus is a program. Draw the boundary between retrieval and generation, and most hybrid problems dissolve.

## The Managed Landscape

| Concern | Azure | AWS | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Managed RAG service | Foundry + Azure AI Search (integrated vectorization) | Bedrock Knowledge Bases | Vertex AI Search / RAG Engine |
| Vector store options | AI Search, Cosmos DB vector | OpenSearch, Aurora pgvector, S3 Vectors | Vector Search, AlloyDB pgvector |
| Embedding models | OpenAI + catalog embeddings | Titan/Cohere embeddings | Gemini embeddings |
| Doc parsing help | Content Understanding / Document Intelligence | Bedrock Data Automation | Document AI |

Managed RAG assumes data and model share a platform. Hybrid architecture begins where that assumption ends.

## The Three Canonical Patterns

- **Colocated RAG** — corpus, index, and model on one platform. Simplest, fastest, cheapest ops. If data and an acceptable model share a cloud (or the model is available in-region on your anchor cloud — check the catalog before architecting around its absence), do this and stop reading.
- **Replicated index** — data is *movable* (classification permits): keep the corpus home, replicate the *vector index* (or a curated, redacted extract) into the model's cloud. Pays one-time-plus-delta egress instead of per-query hops; adds an index-freshness pipeline and a second estate to secure. The workhorse for non-sensitive corpora.
- **Retrieval-at-source** — data *cannot* move: index and retrieval stay in the data's jurisdiction; only the query and top-k snippets cross to the model at generation time. Every query pays cross-boundary latency (network lesson arithmetic applies), and — critically — *snippets still reach the model*. If even that is prohibited, the model must come to the data: in-region endpoints, VPC-confined open-weight models on your own GPUs, or provider sovereign/confidential-inference options. Compliance may also accept snippet-level redaction/anonymization at the boundary — that is a legal conversation, engineered in the retrieval layer.

## Cross-Cutting Realities

- **Identity spans the pipeline.** The retrieval call from cloud B into cloud A's search endpoint should use workload identity federation (identity lesson), not an API key in a config map. RAG pipelines are exactly where cross-cloud service calls proliferate.
- **Permission-aware retrieval is non-negotiable.** The index must carry document ACLs and filter *at query time by the requesting user's entitlements* — otherwise RAG becomes a data-leak amplifier that helpfully summarizes documents the user could never open. This single requirement kills many naive architectures.
- **Latency budget the whole chain.** Embed-query + search + rerank + generate; a cross-cloud hop inside that chain is fine *once*, fatal if the orchestrator chats back and forth per query.
- **Evaluate before and after moving anything.** Retrieval quality (recall@k, groundedness) is the metric that decides whether a redacted/replicated index actually works — architecture reviews should see eval numbers, not just diagrams.

## Real Failure Modes

- **The embedding side-channel.** Team ships PII documents to an out-of-region embedding API "because only vectors come back." The *documents went out* — that is the transfer. Residency review must cover step 2, not just storage.
- **The stale replica.** Replicated index refreshed weekly; policy documents updated Tuesday; the assistant confidently cites the superseded version for six days. Index freshness is an SLO with an owner.
- **The ACL-free index.** Every document embedded into one flat index; the CEO's compensation memo surfaces in an intern's chat. Permission filters must be enforced in the retrieval layer, not by prompt-engineering pleas.
- **The double-embedding bill.** Corpus embedded on cloud A with model X, replicated to cloud B where retrieval uses embedding model Y — the vectors are incompatible; the corpus is embedded twice, forever, on every refresh. Pin the embedding model as part of the *contract* between estates.

## What You Will Build

The challenge implements the placement planner: for each use case — data region, movability, model region — emit the pattern (colocated_rag, replicate_index, or retrieval_at_source). It is the first whiteboard of every enterprise RAG engagement, expressed as twelve lines of Python.

description.en: RAG as five placeable workloads: the boundary between retrieval and generation is where hybrid architectures draw the line — with ACL filtering welded into retrieval.

left.label: Ship the corpus to the model's cloud because the model is there

right.label: Keep data home; send only the query and top-k snippets to the model

description.en: Generation needs snippets, not the corpus. Kilobytes per query cross the boundary instead of terabytes per migration.

explanation.en: Same region and platform reachability: colocate everything and keep life simple — always check this first.
Movable data: replicate the index (not the corpus) into the model's cloud; pay egress once plus deltas, own a freshness SLO.
Immovable data: retrieval stays home; only query and snippets travel — and if even snippets cannot, bring the model to the data.

description.en: Retrieval-at-source: the per-query flow when data cannot leave its jurisdiction.
Four use cases, three patterns — the portfolio view every enterprise RAG program starts from.

instruction.en: Build the hybrid RAG placement planner. Implement plan(data_region, movable, model_region) -> str: 'colocated_rag' if data_region == model_region; else 'replicate_index' if movable is True; else 'retrieval_at_source'. For each (name, data_region, movable, model_region) in USE_CASES (in order) print exactly '<name>: <pattern>'. Then print 'Patterns used: <n>' where n is the count of DISTINCT patterns across the use cases. This decision tree is the first whiteboard of every enterprise RAG engagement.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
USE_CASES = [
    ("support-bot", "eu-west", False, "eu-west"),
    ("sales-assistant", "us-east", True, "eu-west"),
    ("health-records-qa", "eu-central", False, "us-east"),
    ("wiki-search", "ap-south", True, "ap-south"),
]

def plan(data_region, movable, model_region):
    # TODO: colocated_rag / replicate_index / retrieval_at_source per the rules
    pass

# TODO: print '<name>: <pattern>' per use case
# TODO: print 'Patterns used: <distinct pattern count>'
practice_challenges.expected_output:
support-bot: colocated_rag
sales-assistant: replicate_index
health-records-qa: retrieval_at_source
wiki-search: colocated_rag
Patterns used: 3

en: Check region equality FIRST — colocation beats both other patterns even when data is movable.
Only immovable, differently-placed data falls through to retrieval_at_source.
Collect each result in a set while printing, then print f'Patterns used: {len(pattern_set)}'.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert plan("eu-west", False, "eu-west") == "colocated_rag"
assert plan("eu-west", True, "eu-west") == "colocated_rag"
assert plan("us-east", True, "eu-west") == "replicate_index"
assert plan("eu-central", False, "us-east") == "retrieval_at_source"
assert plan("ap-south", True, "ap-south") == "colocated_rag"

q.en: Why does the retrieval/generation boundary matter so much in hybrid RAG?

en: Generation requires the full corpus on every call
Generation needs only the query plus retrieved snippets — kilobytes can cross clouds where terabytes cannot
Vector indexes only exist on one provider

q.en: A team sends PII documents to an out-of-region embedding API, storing only vectors locally. What did the residency review miss?

en: Nothing — vectors are not personal data
The documents themselves crossed the boundary during embedding, and embeddings derived from PII inherit its classification

q.en: Why is permission-aware (ACL-filtered) retrieval non-negotiable in enterprise RAG?

en: Without query-time entitlement filtering, RAG surfaces and summarizes documents the requesting user could never open

q.en: In the replicated-index pattern, what new operational obligation appears?

en: An index freshness SLO and refresh pipeline, plus securing a second estate — with the embedding model pinned across both
